coredata.dc:title: Surface plasmon-enhanced electrochemiluminescence of P, N-doped carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of BRAF gene
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Erli
Ning, Zhenqiang
Yin, Fei
Fang, Zhengzou
Chen, Mengyuan
Zhang, Mingming
Xu, Wenhua
Zhang, Yuanjian
Shen, Yanfei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown enormous potential as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) probes for biosensing and bioimaging applications due to their ease of synthesis and modification, high biocompatibility, stable optical and electrochemical properties. However, the mild ECL emission of CDs often restricts their practical applications. Herein, an ECL biosensor for mutant BRAF gene determination was proposed based on the surface plasmon resonance-enhanced ECL (SPR-ECL) between asymmetrically modified amine-gold nanodendrites (am-AuNDs) and phosphorus (P) and nitrogen (N) co-doped CDs (PNCDs). Compared to the N-doped CDs (NCDs), PNCDs exhibited an 8-fold enhanced ECL because of the larger atom size and higher electron-donating ability of P atom compared to N. Due to the surface plasmon resonance effect of am-AuNDs and proper distance controlled by catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA), the ECL signal of PNCDs was remarkably improved based on a nonradiative energy transfer between PNCDs and the am-AuNDs. The as-constructed SPR-ECL biosensor performed well for the detection of mutant B-type Raf kinase (BRAF) gene with high sensitivity, stability and accuracy in the range from 0.5 pM to 2000 pM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.34 pM. This work would open a new avenue to boost the ECL performance of carbon nanomaterials for practical applications, especially for bioanalysis and diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phosphorus doping
ECL emitter
Surface plasmon resonance
BRAF gene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Covalent immobilization of PNCDs on GCE and fabrication of SPR-ECL biosensor
Detection of BRAF gene in human serum
PL and ECL behavior of PNCDs
Analytical performance of the SPR-ECL biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22074015 and 22174014 ) and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX21_0154 ).

Yanfei Shen received her PhD from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences under the supervision of Professor Li Niu in 2007. After that she moved to Max-Planck Institute of Colloids and Interfaces, Potsdam-Golm, Germany, as a postdoctoral researcher under the guidance of Prof. H. Möhwald. In 2010 she was awarded a JSPS postdoctoral fellowship to work in Dr T. Nakanishi's group at National Institute for Materials Science, Tsukuba, Japan. Currently she is a professor in Southeast University, Nanjing, China. Her current research interests are functional molecular self-assembly and applications for bioanalysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PNCDs with a particle size distribution histogram in the inset, (b) HRTEM image of PNCDs with the corresponding FFT pattern in the inset, (c) FTIR spectra of PNCDs, (d) C1s spectrum, (e) P2p spectrum and (f) N1s spectrum of the high-resolution XPS for PNCDs.

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra (black line), PL excitation spectra (red line), and PL emission spectra (blue line) of PNCDs, λex= 365 nm. (b) ECL-potential curves of GCE/PNCDs (red line), GCE/NCDs (blue line) and bare GCE (black line) in PBS solution (0.1 M, pH=7.5) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. (c) PL (blue line) and ECL spectra (red line) of PNCDs. (d) ECL stability of GCE/PNCDs in PBS solution (0.1 M, pH=7.5) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8.

(a) TEM image of am-AuNDs. (b) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra (black line) of the am-AuNDs and the fitted ECL spectra of PNCDs (red line). (c) CV curves of the SPR-ECL biosensor at each assembly step in 2.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] solution. (d) ECL curves of the SPR-ECL biosensor at each assembly step in PBS solution (0.1 M, pH=7.5) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8.

(a) ECL intensities of the proposed SPR-ECL biosensor with different concentrations of the target BRAF gene in PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.5) solution containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. (b) The linear relationship between the ΔECL and the logarithm of target BRAF gene concentrations. (c) The signal stability of the proposed SPR-ECL biosensor incubated with 100 pM target BRAF gene under continuous scanning for 10 cycles. (d) Bar graph of the ΔECL of the proposed SPR-ECL biosensor hybridized with various DNA sequences. a: PBS, b: 100 pM one-base mismatched DNA sequence (wild-type BRAF gene), c: 100 pM target BRAF gene (mutant BRAF gene), d: the mixture of one base-mismatched DNA sequence and target BRAF gene.
Scheme for the preparation of PNCDs (a) and the construction of the proposed SPR-ECL biosensor for mutant BRAF gene detection (b).
Comparison of the biosensing performance for BRAF gene by different methods.

row.entry: Rolling circle amplification and Förster resonance energy transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of mutant BRAF gene in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Surface plasmon-enhanced electrochemiluminescence of P, N-doped carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of BRAF gene

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Medical School, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown enormous potential as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) probes for biosensing and bioimaging applications due to their ease of synthesis and modification, high biocompatibility, stable optical and electrochemical properties. However, the mild ECL emission of CDs often restricts their practical applications. Herein, an ECL biosensor for mutant BRAF gene determination was proposed based on the surface plasmon resonance-enhanced ECL (SPR-ECL) between asymmetrically modified amine-gold nanodendrites (am-AuNDs) and phosphorus (P) and nitrogen (N) co-doped CDs (PNCDs). Compared to the N-doped CDs (NCDs), PNCDs exhibited an 8-fold enhanced ECL because of the larger atom size and higher electron-donating ability of P atom compared to N. Due to the surface plasmon resonance effect of am-AuNDs and proper distance controlled by catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA), the ECL signal of PNCDs was remarkably improved based on a nonradiative energy transfer between PNCDs and the am-AuNDs. The as-constructed SPR-ECL biosensor performed well for the detection of mutant B-type Raf kinase (BRAF) gene with high sensitivity, stability and accuracy in the range from 0.5 pM to 2000 pM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.34 pM. This work would open a new avenue to boost the ECL performance of carbon nanomaterials for practical applications, especially for bioanalysis and diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a light-emitting process in which the electrochemical oxidation/reduction of the luminophores induces charge transfer between the generated radicals [1,2]. Compared to photoluminescence (PL), ECL does not require an additional light source, thus possessing low background signals. As a result, ECL has been extensively applied for a variety of applications, including biosensing, food and environmental detection, and clinical diagnosis, in which the luminophores play a pivotal role in enhancing the performance [3–7]. Various ECL luminophores such as organic dyes and metal complexes have been reported. However, many of them possess substantial defects, which severely restrain their applications. For instance, the poor water solubility of dyes usually leads to weak ECL signal in aqueous, and the poor biocompatibility of metal complexes limits its applications in the biological system. In this regard, it is still in high demand to explore more highly efficient ECL emitters with high water solubility and biocompatibility for practical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs), as one of the most popular fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, provide a promising alternative to traditional inorganic quantum dots (QDs) such as Cd2+ and Pb2+-based QDs in various applications ranging from bioanalysis to optoelectronics due to their outstanding features, e.g., facile synthesis and surface functionalization, high stability and water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, low cost, stable optical and electrochemical properties [8–10]. Such inherent virtues of CDs offer great opportunities for CDs to be employed as ECL emitters. Since the first discovery of the ECL CDs in 2009 [11], extensive efforts have been devoted to the exploration of high-quality CDs and the enhancement of their ECL emission [12,13]. Particularly, elemental doping has been proven to be one of the most effective strategies to promote the ECL emission of CDs. Pioneering works have shown that nitrogen (N) doping is an effective way to promote the ECL emission of CDs [13,14]. Recently, Ding et al. reported that N- and sulfur (S)- co-doping could induce a near-infrared ECL emission of CDs by introducing new surface states [15]. Phosphorus (P) with the same valence electrons as N has a larger radius of the atom and higher electron-donating ability, thus making it a pretty choice as a dopant for CDs to enhance the ECL performance. Despite these possible virtues of P as a dopant of CDs, it has been never reported regarding the P- and N- co-doped CDs as emitters to enhance the ECL activity.

To fabricate an ECL biosensor with ultra-high sensitivity, coupling the highly efficient ECL emitters with a proper amplification technique was considered as an efficient pathway. Localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), a particular feature of plasmonic materials such as gold (Au) and silver (Ag) nanoparticles, can cause strong oscillation of the conduction electrons at these nanoparticles surface [16–19]. Due to the outstanding ability in signal enhancement, LSPR has been widely applied in various detection techniques such as surface plasmon-enhanced photoluminescence and surface plasmon resonance-enhanced ECL (SPR-ECL) [20,21]. For instance, Au nanoparticles-based plasmon is considered to be highly efficient for enhancing the ECL signal of ruthenium(II)tris(bipyridine) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) and CdTe nanocrystals based on the proper distance control [22,23]. However, the LSPR character of Au nanoparticles has not been applied for the ECL signal enhancement of CDs yet.

Herein, an LSPR effect-involved ECL biosensor was constructed using the P- and N- co-doped CDs (PNCDs) as efficient ECL luminophores and the asymmetrically modified amine-gold nanodendrites (am-AuNDs) as an ECL enhancer based on an LSPR effect. Compared with the N-doped CDs (NCDs), the PNCDs displayed much more enhanced ECL emission. Moreover, the catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) was applied as both a molecular recognition element and a signal amplification unit, and was also employed to regulate the distance between the PNCDs and am-AuNDs. Due to the surface plasmon resonance effect of am-AuNDs and proper distance controlled by CHA, the ECL signal of PNCDs was remarkably improved based on a nonradiative energy transfer between PNCDs and the am-AuNDs. B-type Raf kinase (BRAF) gene, encoding cytoplasmic protein serine/threonine kinases, significantly affects cell division, differentiation, and secretion [24]. Detection of single-base mutations in BRAF gene and related circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) thus can provide important information in cancer diagnosis and treatment [25,26]. Although DNA sequencing and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) are the traditional methods for determining gene mutations, the sequencing method is expensive and needs a long time for analysis, and the PCR method is prone to false positives for the detection of single-base mutations, which make them not desirable for the determination of single-base mutations in BRAF gene [27–29]. In this work, a “signal on” SPR-ECL biosensor for the determination of BRAF gene was proposed ( Scheme 1), which exhibited a wide linear range, low limit of detection (LOD), and high sensitivity, stability and accuracy. This work would offer an extra route for enhancing the ECL performance of carbon nanomaterials in practical applications, especially for bioanalysis and diagnostics.

PNCDs were synthesized through a “one-pot” hydrothermal method (Scheme 1a). Briefly, 0.073 g of 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) was dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water. Then, 0.47 mL of bis[tetrakis(hydroxymethyl)phosphonium] sulfate solution (THPS) was added and sonicated for 30 min. Next, the mixture was poured into the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was purified by centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis. Finally the solid powder of PNCDs was obtained after being collected from the supernatant and freeze-drying. NCDs were synthesized similarly to PNCDs, except that THPS was not added.

Asymmetrically modified amine-gold nanodendrites (am-AuNDs) were synthesized according to previous reports [23,30]. Hairpin DNA2 (H2)-functionalized am-AuNDs (H2 −am-AuNDs) were prepared through an amidation reaction between the carboxyl groups on H2 and amino groups on am-AuNDs. Specifically, 62.5 μL of 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 40 mM) and 62.5 μL of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 20 mM) were mixed with 875 μL of H2 (4 μM) to activate the carboxyl groups of H2, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Then, 500 μL of the as-prepared am-AuNDs was injected into the above solution and incubated for 8 h at 4 °C. After that, the solution was centrifugated at 5000 rpm for 5 min and washed with PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.5), and the precipitate was redispersed in 500 μL of PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.5). The as-obtained H2 −am-AuNDs were stored at 4 °C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent immobilization of PNCDs on GCE and fabrication of SPR-ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 0.3 µm alumina slurry and then ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol and ultrapure water. The fresh GCE was immersed in PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.5) containing 2.5 mg/mL PNCDs and scanned from 0.2 to 1.6 V at 0.1 V/s for 5 cycles for electrochemical grafting of PNCDs onto the GCE surface (GCE/PNCDs). After drying at room temperature, the above GCE/PNCDs was immersed in the mixture solution of EDC (20 mM) and NHS (10 mM), and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. After rinsing with PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.5), the GCE/PNCDs was immersed in the PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.5) solution containing hairpin DNA1 (H1, 4 μM), and incubated for 8 h at 4 °C. Afterward, the modified GCE was blocked with 0.2 wt% BSA solution. Thus, the GCE/PNCDs/H1/BSA was obtained after washing with PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.5). After that, the above GCE/PNCDs/H1/BSA was incubated with the PBS solution containing the as-prepared H2 −am-AuNDs and different concentrations of BRAF gene for 2 h at 37 °C. Finally, the GCE/PNCDs/H1/BSA/BRAF gene/H2 −am-AuNDs was thoroughly washed with PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.5) and dried with pure N2 for further measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BRAF gene in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum was obtained from healthy people and diluted 50-fold with PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.5). The serum samples were spiked with the mutant BRAF gene into different concentrations (1, 10, 100, and 1000 pM). The spiked serum samples were then analyzed according to the calibration curve of the as-proposed SPR-ECL biosensor.

The morphology of the PNCDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1a, PNCDs displayed quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of 4.0 ± 0.1 nm (n = 180 individual particles). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image and fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (Fig. 1b) illustrated the high crystalline carbon structure of PNCDs with the (100), (020), and (002) lattices of the graphitic carbon, indicative of the formation of CDs [31–33]. To gain more information on the chemical composition and functional groups of the PNCDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were further measured. A broad peak centered at 3285 cm−1 in Fig. 1c indicated the presence of amino (−NH2) and hydroxyl (−OH) functional groups on the surface of the PNCDs [34,35]. A typical peak located at 2897 cm−1 can be attributed to the −CH2− stretching vibration [36,37]. The absorption bands at 1750, 1641, 1419, and 1298 cm−1 identified the presence of CO, CC (or CN), −COO− , and C−N (or C−O−C) bonds, respectively [37–39]. Three typical peaks at 1137, 928, and 618 cm−1 corresponded to P − O−C, P − O, and P − C bonds, respectively [40,41]. Thus, the above results suggested that the surface of PNCDs was rich in amino, carboxyl, and phosphorus-containing functional groups, which was critical for enhancing the water dispersibility, and facile for the covalent linking with biomolecules and electrodes.

The FTIR results were supported by the XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. S1, the XPS full-survey spectrum showed that the PNCDs were mainly composed of P, C, N, and O elements. As shown in Fig. 1d, the C1s spectrum which was deconvoluted into four peaks, manifested the presence of aromatic C (284.2 eV), C–N/C–P/C–O (284.9 eV), C–O–P/C–OH (285.7 eV), and COOH (288.2 eV) [42,43]. On the other hand, the P2p spectrum in Fig. 1e was deconvoluted into two peaks at 131.7 and 132.5 eV, which were assigned to the P–C and P–O bonding, respectively [44]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) resolved into three peaks showed the presence of pyridinic N of (398.9 eV), amino N/pyrrolic N (399.8 eV), and graphitic N (401.6 eV) [45,46]. Moreover, the O1s spectrum contained two peaks at 530.5 and 531.9 eV, assigned to OC and O–C, respectively (Fig. S2) [47]. Therefore, both the FTIR and the XPS results revealed that the PNCDs were rich in amino, carboxyl, and phosphorus-containing functional groups. These multiple functional groups endowed PNCDs with excellent water solubility and abundant functional groups for conjugation with GCE and biomolecules, which were critical for enhancing the biosensing performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL and ECL behavior of PNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical properties of PNCDs were studied by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) analysis. As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum ( Fig. 2a), the shoulder peak at 230 nm belongs to the π–π * transition of the aromatic sp2 bond, and the broader absorption peak at about 350 nm could be assigned to the n–π * transition of the CO band [33,48]. The PL spectrum (Fig. 2a) showed the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the PNCDs appeared at 365 nm and 490 nm, while those of NCDs were at 305 nm and 405 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). The difference in PL performance between PNCDs and NCDs could be attributed to the P doping-induced variation of the band structures and surface functional groups. Moreover, the PNCDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission (Fig. S4), indicative of different surface states on PNCDs [34,49].

Taking advantage of the amino groups on PNCDs which can be electrochemically oxidized to cation radicals, PNCDs were electrochemically grafted onto GCE (GCE/PNCDs) through a one-electron oxidation reaction with the formation of C-N bonding [50]. Notably, although the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) would happen in the potential range above 1.0 V, it was found that the electrochemical grafting of PNCDs on GCE in the range of 0.2 − 1.6 V displayed much higher efficiency (Fig. S5). As a control, NCDs were also electrochemically grafted onto GCE (GCE/NCDs) through the same reaction. The ECL behavior of PNCDs-modified GCE (GCE/PNCDs) was investigated in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.5) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 in a range from − 0.2 to − 1.8 V. Fig. S6 shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve of GCE/PNCDs in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.5) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. When scanning from − 0.2 V to − 1.8 V, both K2S2O8 and PNCDs were reduced, producing SO4 •− and PNCDs•−. Subsequently, the reaction between SO4 •− and PNCDs•− generated the excited state of PNCDs (PNCDs*) to emit light [49,51]. With the optimized ratio of the precursors of THPS and ATA for the synthesis of PNCDs (Fig. S7), the GCE/PNCDs showed an 8-fold enhancement of ECL intensity compared with that of GCE/NCDs (Fig. 2b). Moreover, as another control experiment, by using the p-phthalic acid instead of ATA for the synthesis of P-doped CDs, the as-obtained product exhibited negligible ECL emission (Fig. S8). In this sense, N-dopants endow CDs with ECL properties; while the additional P-doping significantly boosted ECL emission of N-doped CDs, which hinted that the P doping played a vital role in enhancing the ECL emission of NCDs. The boosted ECL performance of PNCDs could be ascribed to the surface emissive sites introduced by the P doping [15,44]. Moreover, the ECL emission of PNCDs displayed distinct red-shifted characteristics relative to their corresponding PL emission (Fig. 2c), which could be attributed to the charge injection process in ECL through the surface states. This result confirmed the surface state of PNCDs with abundant reactive groups for ECL emission. In addition, a stable ECL signal (with a relative standard deviation of 0.99%) was observed upon concessive scanning for 20 cycles (Fig. 2d), indicative of sufficient stability of the GCE/PNCDs. These results demonstrated that PNCDs could be an excellent ECL luminophore for biosensing applications.

By taking the mutant BRAF gene as a proof-of-concept analyte, an SPR-ECL biosensor was constructed by using PNCDs as ECL probes and am-AuNDs as a signal intensifier. The am-AuNDs with a uniform diameter size of about 60 nm ( Fig. 3a) displayed a maximum absorption at around 595 nm, which significantly overlapped with the ECL emission of PNCDs (Fig. 3b). Remarkably, the am-AuNDs with sharp tips and edges could lead to a strong electromagnetic field [23]. In principle, by assembling the am-AuNDs onto the PNCDs-modified GCE with a certain distance, the ECL emission of PNCDs would excite the hot electrons of the am-AuNDs into surface plasmon states, and the hot electrons in surface plasmon states would transfer to the conduction band of PNCDs, which would promote the ECL of PNCDs significantly. It should be noted that the asymmetrically modified am-AuNDs enabled the functionalization of amine groups on the top point of am-AuNDs. As a result, the distance between am-AuNDs and PNCDs could be fixed, which would lead to a high reproducibility [23]. Moreover, the CHA was introduced not only as a traditional signal amplification and signal recognition unit but also for the distance control between PNCDs and am-AuNDs for the SPR-enhanced ECL in the biosensor construction [52,53]. Generally, the suitable distance between the ECL emitter and the plasmonic materials for SPR-ECL are about 10 – 20 nm, and the ECL signal was in positive relationship with the distance between PNCDs and the am-AuNDs in this range [23,54]. Since the length of the target DNA base pairs (30 bp) in the current work is about 10 nm, the distance between PNCDs and the am-AuNDs would be longer, when further considering the ring opening of the hairpin DNA1 upon the hybridization with the target DNA. As a result, the distance between PNCDs and the am-AuNDs was optimized to be about 20 nm.

The construction process of the ECL biosensor was shown in Scheme 1b. PNCDs were firstly electrochemically grafted onto GCE by cyclic scanning in PBS solution. The peak current decreased quickly with the successive scanning and became stable after five cycles (Fig. S9), indicative of the successful grafting of PNCDs on the GCE surface [55]. The hairpin DNA probe H1 was covalently linked with PNCDs through an amidation reaction. The presence of the target BRAF gene would cause the initiation of the CHA reaction, i.e., the H1 stretched to a rodlike shape to hybridize with the target. Since the sequences of the exposed domain of the H1 were partly complementary to the toehold domain of the H2-am-AuNDs, the addition of the H2−am-AuNDs can trigger another branch migration, which displaced the target BRAF gene, inducing the recycling of the target for signal amplification. On the other hand, the hybridization resulted in the formation of a duplex of “H1−H2−am-AuNDs”, reaching to a proper distance between am-AuNDs and PNCDs [52,54], which would cause an ECL signal enhancement of PNCDs based on an LSPR effect. The promoted ECL signal would be highly desirable for the quantitative detection of the target BRAF gene.

The feasibility of the designed CHA system was verified with a PAGE electrophoresis analysis (Fig. S10), and the stepwise construction of the ECL biosensor was firstly verified by cyclic voltammetry (CV). As shown in Fig. 3c, a pair of redox peaks of K3[Fe(CN)6] at the bare GCE was observed. After the electrochemical grafting of PNCDs onto the electrode, the peak current decreased slightly. Upon stepwisely immobilizing H1, BSA, and the target BRAF gene and H2−am-AuNDs, the peak current decreased gradually, ascribing to the hindered electron transfer from K3[Fe(CN)6] to the electrode surface, illustrating the successful fabrication of the biosensor. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also employed to investigate the interfacial properties during the stepwise assembly for the biosensor fabrication. The EIS results (Fig. S11 and Table S2) showed that the Rct increased gradually after PNCDs, H1, BSA and H2-am-AuNDs were assembled onto the electrode, which further confirmed the successful fabrication of the biosensor. The successful assembly of the biosensor was also proved by the ECL intensity variation after each assembly step. As revealed in Fig. 3d, the bare GCE in the K2S2O8 solution showed a negligible ECL signal. After grafting PNCDs onto the electrode, the ECL intensity increased obviously. After the immobilization of H1 and BSA onto the electrode surface, the ECL signal decreased slightly. With the subsequent presence of the target BRAF gene and H2−am-AuNDs, the ECL signal elevated significantly, which could be attributed to the plasmon resonance effect of am-AuNDs. All the results demonstrated that the proposed SPR-ECL biosensor was successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the SPR-ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S12 − Fig. S14), the analytical performance of the SPR-ECL biosensor for different concentrations of the target BRAF gene was assayed. Fig. 4a shows that the ECL intensity increased gradually with the increase of the target BRAF gene concentration. As shown in Fig. 4b, there was an excellent linear relationship between the variation of ECL intensity (ΔECL) and the logarithm of the target BRAF gene concentrations in the range from 0.5 to 2000 pM, with a detection limit of 0.34 pM (S/N = 3). The linear regression equation was expressed as Y= 1419.3 LogC + 704.4 with R2 = 0.998. Compared with those in previous reports, the proposed SPR-ECL biosensor displayed superior sensitivity and a broader detection range ( Table 1). Moreover, the as-established ECL biosensor exhibited high stability with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.65% after consecutive scanning for 10 cycles (Fig. 4c).

For the selectivity evaluation, the proposed biosensor was incubated with the blank PBS solution, the target BRAF gene, the one base-mismatched DNA, and the mixture, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4d, the ECL response to the blank PBS solution and the one base-mismatched DNA was much lower than those to the target BRAF gene and the mixture, demonstrating that the interfering DNA would not cause an inappreciable interference for the detection. To evaluate the feasibility of this SPR-ECL biosensor, four spiked human serum samples containing different concentrations (1, 10, 100, and 1000 pM) of the mutant BRAF gene were detected. As displayed in Table 2, according to the standard calibration plot in Fig. 4b, the recovery was calculated to be in a range of 97.83 – 102.87%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 7%, showing excellent reliability for the detection of the mutant BRAF gene in real samples.

In summary, a highly sensitive SPR-ECL biosensor was designed for the detection of mutant BRAF gene by using P- and N-co-doped CDs as efficient ECL emitters and am-AuNDs as ECL signal enhancers based on a localized surface plasmon effect. The P- and N-co-doped CDs presented an 8-fold enhancement of the ECL signal compared to the N-doped CDs due to the larger atom size and higher electron-donating property of P. The surface plasmon resonance from am-AuNDs caused a significantly enhanced ECL signal of the PNCDs emitters, which was based on a nonradiative energy transfer between PNCDs and the am-AuNDs. In addition, the CHA was applied not only as the recognition and amplification element but also to control the proper distance between the am-AuNDs and the PNCDs emitters for the LSPR-based ECL enhancement. As a result, the SPR-ECL biosensor displayed a wide linear range, high sensitivity and stability, and satisfactory accuracy, demonstrating a promising prospect for future practical applications especially for biosensing and diagnostics.

Erli Yang: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Zhenqiang Ning: Formal analysis, Investigation. Fei Yin: Formal analysis. Zhengzou Fang: Formal analysis. Mengyuan Chen: Formal analysis. Mingming Zhang: Formal analysis. Wenhua Xu: Formal analysis. Yuanjian Zhang: Formal analysis. Yanfei Shen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074015 and 22174014) and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX21_0154).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132288.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable plasmon-assisted electrochemiluminescence strategy for determination of the rapidly accelerated fibrosarcoma B-type (BRAF) gene using concave gold nanocubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhang, Z. Liang, Y. Nie, X. Zhang, Q. Ma, Tunable plasmon-assisted electrochemiluminescence strategy for determination of the rapidly accelerated fibrosarcoma B-type (BRAF) gene using concave gold nanocubes, Microchimica Acta 187(2020) 599.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Erli Yang received his MS degree of analytical chemistry (2019) at Nanning Normal University and is currently working with Prof. Yanfei Shen for his PhD. His current research interests are the synthesis of carbon dots and their application in electrochemiluminescence biosensing.
Zhenqiang Ning received his PhD from Southeast University in 2021. His research interests focus on analytical electrochemiluminescence in biomedical field.
Fei Yin received her MS from Liaocheng University in 2020 and is currently working with Prof. Yanfei Shen for her PhD degree. Her research interests focus on electrochemiluminescence biosensors.
Zhengzou Fang received his PhD from Southeast University in 2022. His research interests focus on nanomaterial, nanocarrier and environment-responsive hydrogel.
Mengyuan Chen is currently working with Prof. Yanfei Shen for her MS degree. Her research interests focus on synthesis and applications of nanoclusters.
Mingming Zhang received her MS from Southeast University in 2018 and is currently working in the Center of Clinical Laboratory Medicine, Zhongda Hospital, Southeast University.

Wenhua Xu received her PhD from Ocean University of China and worked in Qingdao University. At present, she is a professor and has become a doctoral tutor and postdoctoral mentor. In 2022, she was selected as the distinguished Taishan scholar and the outstanding teacher of innovation and entrepreneurship of Shandong Province. She is on working in Oxford University as a senior visiting scholar. One of her research directions is focused on the repair of microenvironment of severe tissue injury, and she has developed a series of tissue engineering carriers using natural marine extracts, which can transfer a variety of cells and drugs for the treatment of severe tissue injuries such as skin burns and corneal alkali burns, etc. The other research direction is engaged in gene targeted diagnosis and precision therapy.

Yuanjian Zhang received his BS from Nanjing University in 2002 and completed his PhD in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences under the supervision of Prof. L. Niu in 2007. After that, he joined Prof. M. Antonietti's group at MPI of Colloids and Interfaces (Germany) as a postdoctoral researcher. In October 2009, he moved to National Institute for Materials Science, Japan as an ICYS researcher. He is currently a professor in Southeast University, China. His research interests include, but are not limited to, functional carbon-rich materials with electrocatalytic and photocatalytic properties for biosensing.

Yanfei Shen received her PhD from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences under the supervision of Professor Li Niu in 2007. After that she moved to Max-Planck Institute of Colloids and Interfaces, Potsdam-Golm, Germany, as a postdoctoral researcher under the guidance of Prof. H. Möhwald. In 2010 she was awarded a JSPS postdoctoral fellowship to work in Dr T. Nakanishi's group at National Institute for Materials Science, Tsukuba, Japan. Currently she is a professor in Southeast University, Nanjing, China. Her current research interests are functional molecular self-assembly and applications for bioanalysis.
